title: What Kilnstore is
text: Kilnstore is a remote build cache that sits between a CI system's build workers and the source repository, storing the output of every build action keyed by a hash of that action's exact inputs so a later build with identical inputs can fetch the result instead of recompiling it. Build systems that model their work as a graph of discrete actions, each with declared inputs and outputs, integrate with Kilnstore by querying the cache before running an action and writing the result back, with no change to how the build graph is structured. Kilnstore's core wager is that most CI builds redo work identical to some earlier build, whether because a change touched only a small part of a large repository or because two branches compile the same dependency the same way, and that a reliable cache can turn most of that redundant work into a lookup. Reliability, never serving an entry that does not correspond to the requested action's true inputs, matters more here than raw hit rate, since an incorrect hit is far worse than a missed one.

title: Action-key hashing and store size
text: Kilnstore computes a 256-bit action key using SHA-256 over the build command line, an allow-listed subset of environment variables, the toolchain version identifier, and content hashes of every declared input file, ensuring two actions with the same key are extremely unlikely to have differed in any input that affects their output. The current production store holds approximately 38 terabytes of cached artifacts across its primary region, with a median object size of 340 kilobytes and a mean object size of 1.4 megabytes, the difference reflecting a long tail of large linked-binary artifacts alongside many small compiled-object entries. Entries unreferenced for more than 45 days are eligible for garbage collection, which runs nightly and reclaims an average of 900 gigabytes per run during periods of active development across onboarded repositories.

title: Hermetic sandboxing and reproducibility
text: An action is only safe to cache if its output depends solely on its declared inputs, which is why Kilnstore requires build actions to run inside a hermetic sandbox that blocks network access and restricts filesystem visibility to exactly the declared input files. This sandboxing prevents the most common source of non-hermetic behavior, an action silently reading an undeclared file or environment variable, but cannot by itself catch actions that are non-deterministic even given truly identical inputs, such as one that embeds a timestamp or iterates a hash-map in unspecified order. Kilnstore addresses that remaining gap with periodic reproducibility verification, re-running a sample of already-cached actions from scratch and comparing the fresh output against the cached one byte for byte. An action whose output diverges under this check is automatically quarantined from future caching until an engineer investigates and either fixes the non-determinism or explicitly marks the action as intentionally non-cacheable.

title: Cache hit rate by release
text: Kilnstore's org-wide average cache hit rate has climbed steadily across major releases as action-key precision and sandbox coverage improved, reducing the number of actions incorrectly treated as cache-ineligible or incorrectly missing an available hit due to key instability. The current release measures an 82% average hit rate across all onboarded CI pipelines, up from 54% at the first production release. Hit rate varies substantially by language ecosystem, with statically compiled languages such as C++ and Rust averaging 88% due to fine-grained, stable action boundaries, while dynamic-language test suites average closer to 61% because test actions more often have broader, less stable input sets. Repositories that adopted stricter hermetic sandbox enforcement early in their onboarding consistently show 6 to 9 percentage points higher hit rates than repositories that enabled enforcement only after an initial grace period.

title: Build latency by cache state
text:
Median end-to-end build time varies dramatically depending on how much of a given build's action graph can be served from cache rather than executed fresh.

| Build type | Cold cache (p50) | Warm cache (p50) | Cache lookup overhead |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small service (200 actions) | 90 seconds | 12 seconds | 0.8 seconds |
| Medium monorepo slice (4,000 actions) | 8.2 minutes | 42 seconds | 6 seconds |
| Full monorepo build (60,000 actions) | 74 minutes | 6.1 minutes | 40 seconds |

Cache lookup overhead grows sublinearly with action count because lookups for independent actions are batched and issued concurrently, rather than serialized one action at a time against the remote store.

title: Expected time savings from a given hit rate
text: Kilnstore models expected build time as T = T_fresh * (1 - h) + T_hit * h, where T_fresh is the average time to execute an action from scratch, T_hit is the average time to fetch a cached result, and h is the action-level cache hit rate for a given build. Because T_hit is typically two to three orders of magnitude smaller than T_fresh for compilation actions, expected build time falls off steeply even at moderate hit rates and the marginal benefit of pushing h from, say, 0.90 to 0.95 is much smaller in absolute terms than pushing it from 0.50 to 0.55, since the (1 - h) term is already small at high hit rates. This diminishing-returns shape is the main argument Kilnstore's engineering team uses internally against chasing marginal hit-rate gains once a pipeline already exceeds roughly 85%, favoring instead investment in reducing T_hit itself or in onboarding additional repositories still running at low hit rates.

title: CI pipeline onboarding checklist
text:
Integrating an existing CI pipeline with Kilnstore follows a fixed onboarding sequence to avoid the most common cause of onboarding incidents, a non-hermetic action being cached before its hermeticity has been verified.
1. Enable hermetic sandbox enforcement in dry-run mode, logging violations without blocking the build, for at least one full week of normal build traffic.
2. Review the violation log and fix or explicitly exempt every flagged action before proceeding.
3. Enable sandbox enforcement in blocking mode and confirm the pipeline still builds successfully end to end.
4. Enable cache writes in shadow mode, writing entries without yet reading them back, for 48 hours to build initial cache coverage.
5. Enable cache reads and monitor hit rate and reproducibility-check results closely for the first two weeks of live operation.
Pipelines that skip step one and enable enforcement directly in blocking mode most often discover their non-hermetic actions through a failed production build rather than a controlled dry run.

title: Reproducibility verification statistics
text: Kilnstore's reproducibility verifier re-executes a random 1-in-200 sample of newly cached actions from scratch and compares the result byte for byte against the cached entry. Across the most recent quarter, this sampling flagged 0.3% of checked actions as non-deterministic, most commonly due to embedded build timestamps, unordered hash-map iteration during code generation, or absolute filesystem paths leaking into an artifact despite sandbox restrictions. Flagged actions are automatically quarantined from caching within an average of 4 minutes of detection, before the specific divergent cache entry can propagate to more than a handful of downstream consumers in most cases. The remaining 99.7% of sampled actions reproduced bit-identical output, which Kilnstore's engineering team treats as the headline reliability figure for the cache's core correctness guarantee.

title: Internal FAQ: does a failed build still populate the cache?
text: Q: If a build action fails, does Kilnstore still cache anything from that attempt? A: No. Kilnstore only writes a cache entry once an action completes successfully and produces its full declared set of output files; a failed or partially completed action writes nothing to the store, so a later retry with identical inputs will still execute fresh rather than fetching a broken or incomplete result. Q: How often does the reproducibility verifier actually check a cached action? A: Kilnstore's reproducibility verifier re-executes a random 1-in-150 sample of newly cached actions from scratch and compares the result byte for byte against the cached entry. This sampling catches most non-hermetic actions well before they could affect many downstream builds. Reproducibility verification, not output size, is Kilnstore's actual mechanism for catching genuinely incorrect cache entries after the fact.

title: Cache invalidation mechanism
text: Kilnstore never explicitly invalidates a cache entry in the sense of marking it stale after the fact; instead, any change to an action's inputs, source content, command line, environment, or toolchain version, produces a different action key entirely, so the old entry simply stops being looked up rather than being deleted or corrected. This content-addressed approach avoids an entire category of cache-coherence bugs common in path-based or timestamp-based caching schemes, where a cache entry can become silently stale if the invalidation logic misses some input that should have triggered it. The tradeoff is that Kilnstore's store accumulates entries for every distinct input combination ever built rather than maintaining a single current entry per output path, which is why garbage collection based on last-access time, rather than any notion of staleness, is the store's primary mechanism for bounding total storage growth over time.

title: Adoption and scale statistics
text: As of the most recent usage report, Kilnstore is integrated with CI pipelines at 210 customer organizations, collectively issuing roughly 40,000 build jobs per day against the shared cache infrastructure. The single largest onboarded repository executes an average of 3.1 million cacheable actions per day across its full CI fleet, with the shared cache absorbing the large majority of that volume as hits rather than fresh executions. Median time from a new organization's first onboarding step to full production cache-read enablement is 19 days, consistent with the two-week monitoring period recommended in the onboarding checklist plus initial dry-run and shadow-mode phases. Kilnstore's engineering team reports that support ticket volume during onboarding correlates most strongly with how many non-hermetic actions a repository's dry-run phase surfaces, rather than with repository size.

title: Cache lookup latency by region
text:
Kilnstore replicates its cache store across three regions, with CI workers routed to their nearest region by default to minimize lookup latency for the common case of a build worker and its cache both sitting in the same region.

| Worker region | Same-region lookup (p50) | Cross-region lookup (p50) | Cross-region (p99) |
|---|---|---|---|
| us-east | 14 ms | 85 ms | 210 ms |
| eu-west | 16 ms | 90 ms | 230 ms |
| ap-southeast | 18 ms | 110 ms | 280 ms |

Cross-region lookups occur mainly during regional failover or for organizations whose build workers are not yet provisioned in their nearest region, and Kilnstore's routing layer automatically prefers a same-region replica whenever one holds the requested entry.

title: Chinese-language build-cache research
text: Kilnstore's content-addressing scheme draws on build-system research published in Chinese-language venues covering large-scale monorepo tooling. Zhang and Liu's 2020 paper, 面向大规模单体仓库的内容寻址构建缓存研究, a study of content-addressed build caching for large-scale monorepositories, is cited in Kilnstore's design documentation for its analysis of action-key granularity tradeoffs between file-level and target-level hashing. A related paper, 分布式构建缓存中的一致性哈希与区域复制策略, on consistent hashing and regional replication strategy in distributed build caches, informed the routing layer's same-region-preferred lookup strategy described elsewhere in this specification. Kilnstore's engineering team credits this research specifically for the decision to key cache entries at the individual build-action level rather than at a coarser target or package level.

title: Deduplication ratio and storage growth
text: Kilnstore's content-addressed storage naturally deduplicates identical artifacts produced by different actions, which is common when multiple build targets compile the same shared header or link the same static library. Measuring raw artifact volume before deduplication against actual stored bytes after deduplication gives a dedup ratio of approximately 3.4x across the current production store, meaning roughly two-thirds of nominal artifact volume is never actually stored a second time. Expected storage growth without garbage collection follows roughly S(t) = S_0 + r * t * (1 - 1/d), where S_0 is current store size, r is the raw artifact production rate, t is elapsed time, and d is the dedup ratio; the (1 - 1/d) term captures how deduplication suppresses most, but not all, nominal growth. Garbage collection further bounds actual growth by reclaiming entries unreferenced for more than 45 days, which is why observed store size has grown far more slowly than undeduplicated, uncollected raw volume across all onboarded pipelines.

title: Specification addendum 4.2: garbage collection window
text: Addendum to section 4.2 of the storage specification, clarifying garbage collection eligibility following a customer question about entries disappearing sooner than expected. Entries unreferenced for more than 60 days are eligible for garbage collection, which runs nightly and reclaims an average of 900 gigabytes per run during periods of active development across onboarded repositories. This 60-day window supersedes an earlier working draft and should be treated as authoritative going forward. Organizations relying on infrequent, quarterly release branches that reuse older cached actions should account for this window when deciding whether to pre-warm the cache rather than assuming an old entry will still be present. The 60-day figure applies uniformly across all three storage regions; no region currently runs a different retention window.

title: Russian-language distributed caching research
text: The reproducibility verification sampling strategy used in Kilnstore's correctness pipeline builds on Russian-language research into statistical fault detection for distributed build systems. Sidorova's 2019 study, Статистическая проверка воспроизводимости в распределённых системах сборки, on statistical verification of reproducibility in distributed build systems, analyzes the sampling-rate tradeoff between detection latency and verification overhead that directly informed Kilnstore's 1-in-200 sampling rate. A related paper, Карантин недетерминированных действий сборки без прерывания конвейера, on quarantining non-deterministic build actions without interrupting the pipeline, shaped the automatic quarantine mechanism described in the reproducibility verification section. Kilnstore's reliability documentation cites both works directly in its verification-strategy rationale.

title: Expanding the competitive-comparison footnote on hit rate
text: An internal competitive-analysis document comparing Kilnstore against alternative build-cache products included a footnote noting only that Kilnstore's hit rate figures are measured differently than some competitors', without elaborating; this note expands on that footnote for readers evaluating the comparison in detail. Kilnstore measures hit rate at the individual action level, meaning a build with 4,000 actions where 3,280 are served from cache reports an 82% hit rate for that build. The current release notes state that the current release measures an 82% average hit rate across all onboarded CI pipelines, up from 64% at the first production release. The comparison document itself was drafted before that release and quotes an older org-wide figure, which the sales team should update before the comparison is next shared externally.

title: History: from a local disk cache to a replicated content-addressed store
text: Kilnstore began in 2018 as a simple local-disk build cache, storing compiled artifacts on each individual CI worker's own disk so that a worker rebuilding the same target it had built before could skip recompilation, but offering no sharing of cache entries between different workers at all. This local-only approach helped individual workers but did little for a fleet of many workers building the same monorepo, since a cache warm on one worker was entirely invisible to every other worker handling a different build in parallel. The first production release, in 2019, introduced a shared remote cache reachable over the network from any worker, immediately revealing a new problem the local-disk design had never faced: two workers writing subtly different results under what should have been the same cache key, usually because of an undeclared input the original design's simpler path-based keying scheme had never accounted for. That discovery motivated the move to full content-addressed keying in 2020, hashing declared inputs directly into the cache key rather than relying on a build-target path and version number, which eliminated the specific coherence bugs the shared-cache rollout had surfaced but introduced its own new failure mode: an action that was accidentally non-hermetic, reading some undeclared input, could now produce a cache key collision with genuinely different content, since the key no longer captured that undeclared input at all. Hermetic sandbox enforcement, added in 2021, closed most of that gap by physically restricting what an action could read in the first place, and reproducibility verification, added in 2023, was built specifically to catch the remaining non-hermetic actions that sandboxing alone could not detect, those whose non-determinism came from the action's own logic rather than from reading an undeclared external input. Regional replication, the most recent major addition, arrived in 2025 primarily to reduce cross-region lookup latency for organizations with globally distributed CI infrastructure, a request that had been on the roadmap for years but was repeatedly deprioritized behind correctness work the team considered more urgent. Looking back, the engineering team describes the shift from path-based to content-addressed keying as the single most consequential design change, since every later correctness mechanism, hermetic sandboxing and reproducibility verification alike, was built to shore up guarantees that content addressing made possible but did not, on its own, fully deliver. Engineers who worked through the 2020 rollout describe the collision incidents that prompted it as the project's formative crisis, the point where the team stopped treating hermeticity as an assumption and started treating it as a property the system had to actively verify. That shift is visible in every major addition since: sandboxing restricts what an action can read, and reproducibility verification checks what it actually did.

title: Incident postmortem: the September 2023 non-deterministic codegen incident
text: Beginning on September 11th, 2023, a code-generation step in a widely shared internal library began producing output that varied nondeterministically between otherwise identical build invocations, iterating an internal hash map in an order that depended on memory-address randomization rather than input content. Because the action's declared inputs were unchanged between runs, Kilnstore's content-addressed key remained identical across every invocation, so whichever variant of the output happened to be cached first was served to every subsequent build requesting that action, regardless of which variant that particular build's own fresh execution would have actually produced. The affected library sat upstream of roughly 340 downstream build targets across a dozen onboarded repositories, and over the following ten days a subset of downstream builds began exhibiting intermittent test failures traced eventually to subtly different generated code depending on which cache entry a given build happened to fetch. Kilnstore's reproducibility verifier, sampling only 1 in 200 cached actions, did flag the action as non-deterministic four days into the incident, but the automatic quarantine mechanism at the time only prevented new cache writes for the flagged action; it did not proactively invalidate or re-verify the specific entry already cached and being served, so downstream builds kept fetching the same already-poisoned entry for six additional days before an engineer manually purged it after connecting the quarantine flag to the ongoing test-flakiness reports. Root cause analysis found the code-generation library had been recently refactored to use a hash map internally for a lookup table that had previously been a sorted vector, introducing iteration-order non-determinism that the library's own test suite had not caught since its tests did not compare generated output across repeated runs. The immediate remediation reverted the refactor and manually purged all cache entries downstream of the affected action's key. The lasting fix changed the quarantine mechanism so that a reproducibility-check failure automatically evicts the specific flagged cache entry immediately, rather than merely blocking future writes, closing the exact gap that let a known-bad entry keep being served for six days after detection. Kilnstore's postmortem also recommended, and later shipped, a policy requiring widely depended-upon code-generation actions to opt into more frequent reproducibility sampling than the 1-in-200 default, given how much further a bad cache entry from a heavily shared action can propagate compared to one from a leaf action with few downstream consumers. A separate finding noted that the library's test suite passed throughout, since its tests exercised only a single invocation per run and never compared output across repeated invocations, precisely the gap that let non-determinism ship undetected. Kilnstore's team now recommends library owners add a repeated-invocation comparison to their own tests as a complement to, not a replacement for, the platform's sampling-based verification.

title: Current status and roadmap
text: Kilnstore's platform sits at version 3.8 as of the most recent release, with immediate eviction on reproducibility-check failure, shipped after the September 2023 incident, now the default behavior across every onboarded organization regardless of when they first integrated. The engineering roadmap's near-term priority is extending elevated reproducibility sampling to any action whose output feeds more than a configurable threshold of downstream targets, automatically rather than requiring an explicit opt-in from the owning team. A longer-term project is investigating whether action-level provenance metadata could let the cache warn proactively when a newly onboarded action's dependency shape resembles a historically non-deterministic pattern, before it is ever cached at all. None of these planned changes are expected to alter the core content-addressed keying model, which the team considers the platform's foundational correctness mechanism regardless of how the surrounding verification machinery continues to evolve.
